quote: Hey, Don. This is Tyson Ion. I received a request to look at solar for the home.
stewart_read: The rep named Ion and referenced the solar request, which is a partial legitimacy anchor. However, the codex intro template calls for Tesla/state/certified-installer framing to answer 'is this a scam?' immediately. That framing was absent. The gap is the specific legitimacy scaffold, not a total absence of company context — Ion was named, a design-framing followed at 00:19. Strategic-vs-reactive: no evidence this was a deliberate choice to skip the Tesla frame; the reactive read holds — the rep defaulted to a lighter intro without the strongest available legitimacy signal.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been on a non-Tesla lead source and correctly omitted the Tesla frame — but no lead-type metadata is available to confirm this.
coaching_implication: Ask Tyson which intro variant he used and why. If this was a Tesla-sourced lead, walk through the codex intro.tesla template and the legitimacy value of the certified-installer framing. If it was a standard lead, confirm the standard intro template was followed. Either way, the 'I received a request' phrasing is weaker than 'the form you filled out online' — specificity reduces suspicion.

quote: We've thought about it before. We just when this came up saying that they need needed people to test them. Maybe.
stewart_read: Don's answer to 'what got you interested in solar?' is thin and hedged — 'maybe' is the closing word. The codex flags this as the emotional anchor of the call and calls for 60+ seconds of depth-probing. The rep's follow-up ('is this kind of your first time fully getting a design') is a binary clarifier, not a depth probe. The rep checked the box and moved on. This is not a knowledge_gap (the rep lacking an answer) — it is a depth-probe miss on a thin customer answer that needed expansion.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have read Don's hedged answer as low-interest and chosen to move quickly to qualification rather than risk losing him on a long emotional probe. Reactive read holds — the codex is explicit that depth matters more than the answer, and 'maybe' is precisely the signal that warrants a follow-up, not a pivot.
coaching_implication: Show Tyson the codex note: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Don's 'maybe' and 'we've thought about it before' are openings, not dead ends. A suggested probe: 'What's been the thing that's held you back from pulling the trigger on it before?' — that question surfaces bill pain or hesitation without fabricating numbers.

quote: There's nothing in this is there's a new house in the house. And it would be going on the garage, not my house.
stewart_read: Don volunteers two high-value signals in one breath: (1) it's a new construction, meaning the roof is new and solar-ready, and (2) the install target is the garage, not the main house — a detail that changes the design scope entirely. The rep acknowledged the new-home detail and asked a clarifying question, which is adequate. What the rep did NOT do is anchor on the new-roof signal as a positive qualifier or use the garage detail to deepen curiosity ('a garage install is actually a great setup — let me make sure we get the right design for that'). The enthusiasm signal was received but not amplified.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to show Tyson what an enthusiasm signal looks like and how to amplify it. The new construction + garage combination is a strong qualifier — new roof, no shading concern (trees confirmed gone), and a customer who has already thought through the placement. A suggested bridge: 'A brand-new garage roof is actually ideal for this — no re-roof concerns, clean install. Let me make sure we get the right info so the design fits that setup.' That line deepens engagement without scope creep.

quote: Yeah. The trees are not even there.
stewart_read: Don confirms the trees are gone — the shading concern the rep raised is eliminated. This is a second enthusiasm signal: improved sun exposure on a new-construction garage roof. The rep's response was to exit the call on a callback promise rather than treating this as a green light to continue qualification. The imagery gap is real, but it does not block collection of bill, utility, co-owners, credit, or roof age — all of which were still available.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep may have judged that without accurate imagery, the design team cannot proceed and a callback is the correct next step. Rejected — the codex verify section does not require imagery to complete qualification. The rep could have continued and flagged the imagery update as a parallel task.
coaching_implication: This is the call's pivot point. Walk Tyson through what was still collectible after the imagery gap surfaced: bill amount, utility company, co-owners on title, credit qualifier, roof age. The imagery is a design-team problem, not a qualification-stopper. The coaching question: 'What would you have needed to keep going?' — that surfaces whether Tyson understood the verify section's independence from the design imagery.

quote: And then I can give you a callback once I have more updated imagery. Does that work for you?
stewart_read: The rep exits with a vague callback — no locked time, no date, no button_up elements completed. The codex button_up section requires: appointment_datetime_confirmed, one_hour_block_stated, phone_appointment_specified, no_driving_at_computer, is_that_fair_handshake, and text_followup_promise. Zero of these six elements were executed. The wrong-name close ('Appreciate your time for now, Doug') is a minor note on top of a structural button_up failure. Even for a callback rather than a full appointment, locking a time and sending a follow-up text are the minimum retention moves.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have intended to call back quickly and treated this as a brief pause rather than a full close. Reactive read holds — 'Does that work for you?' with no time anchor is a soft close that produces high no-show rates on the callback.
coaching_implication: Pull up the button_up section with Tyson and walk through all six required elements. Then ask: 'If you were going to call Don back, what would you have said to lock that callback?' The goal is a specific time ('I'll call you back Thursday at 2 — does that work?') plus a text confirmation. The wrong name ('Doug' vs 'Don') is worth a quick note but should not be the headline — the structural absence of all button_up elements is the priority.

quote: K. Sounds good. Appreciate your time for now, Doug. You have a good day.
stewart_read: The rep closes by calling Don 'Doug' — a name error on a two-minute call where the customer's name was confirmed at 00:02. This is a minor rapport note, but it lands at the worst possible moment: the last impression before a callback that has no locked time. The name error compounds the structural button_up failure rather than standing alone.
coaching_implication: Brief note only — don't let this become the coaching headline. Mention it as a habit check ('customer's name was confirmed at the top of the call — worth a quick glance at your notes before you close') and move the coaching time to the button_up structural gap.

quote: No. I didn't realize it called you that fast. Well, yeah, as I mentioned, we've seen that request online. I'll keep it pretty short for you.
stewart_read: The rep's acknowledgment of the fast callback ('I didn't realize it called you that fast') is a small transparency moment that could have been a micro-trade — 'I know this is fast, here's exactly what I need from you and how long it takes.' Instead, 'I'll keep it pretty short for you' is a vague time anchor. The codex time_constraint protocol calls for a firm time anchor ('It's a 30-minute call — we'll get you in and out'). The rep's version is softer and less committal, which leaves Don without a clear expectation of the call's scope.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been trying to reduce friction by not over-committing to a time. Reactive read holds — vague time anchors increase no-show and drop-off rates; the codex is explicit on this.
coaching_implication: Show Tyson the time_constraint protocol. The fast-callback acknowledgment was actually a good instinct — it's honest and humanizing. The coaching move is to attach a firm time anchor to it: 'I know this is fast — I just need about 5 minutes to grab a few details and I'll get out of your hair.' That line honors the transparency and sets a concrete expectation.

quote: No. It's first time. First time.
stewart_read: Don confirms this is his first time getting a solar design — a meaningful qualifier that the rep acknowledged and moved past without anchoring. First-time customers have no competitive quote to compare against, which is a significant advantage for Ion. The rep's response was to look at the satellite imagery rather than using the first-time signal to deepen curiosity or set expectations for the design process.
coaching_implication: First-time customer with no competing quotes is a high-value signal. A suggested bridge: 'Perfect — that means we get to show you what a real design looks like from scratch, not just a comparison.' That line creates curiosity and positions Ion as the reference point. Worth a brief note in the 1-on-1 as an example of a positive qualifier that went unanchored.
